title: Thermodynamic Formalism for B-free Dynamical Systems
speaker: Joana Kulaga-Przymus
abstract: Given $B \subset N$, we consider the corresponding set $FB$ of $B$-free integers, i.e. $n \in FB i_ no b \in B$ divides $n$. We $de_{ne} X \eta_}$ the B-free subshift _ as the smallest subshift containing $\eta := 1FB \in {0, 1}Z$. Such systems are interesting both from the dynamical and number theoretical viewpoint and can manifest various types of behavior. I will concentrate on the entropy and its generalization _ topological pressure, with some applications in combinatorics / number theory. Talk will be based on a joint work with Michael Lemanczyk and Michael Rams.
tags: Workshop on Dynamics, Discrete Analysis and Multiplicative Number Theory

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic B-free setup and density taxonomy
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines multiples M_B, B-free sets F_B, primitive generators, natural and logarithmic density, finite periodic approximation, and the main arithmetic classes such as Erdős, Behrend, and taut sets.

proof_stages.label: Symbolic models and arithmetic applications
proof_stages.summary: The characteristic B-free sequence eta is used to build the B-free, hereditary, and admissible subshifts, and these dynamical models are then connected to arithmetic pattern repetition and recurrence theorems.

proof_stages.label: Topological structure of B-free subshifts
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains why Behrend cases are difficult and then characterizes proximality, minimality, essential minimality, and Toeplitz regularity of x_eta in terms of pairwise-coprime subsets and scaled copies inside B.

proof_stages.label: Invariant measures and convolution structure for hereditary closures
proof_stages.summary: Mirsky measures arise from lower-density subsequences, all invariant measures on the hereditary closure are obtained by coordinatewise multiplication, taut reductions preserve these measure data, and the resulting convolution-system structure yields intrinsic ergodicity and entropy formulas.

proof_stages.label: Invariant measures for non-hereditary B-free systems
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the minimal sequence eta-star is regular, the invariant measures on x_eta are described through a three-coordinate averaging map, leading to the abstract notion of biconvolution systems and parallel intrinsic-ergodicity consequences.

proof_stages.label: Thermodynamic formalism up to one-coordinate potentials
proof_stages.summary: After introducing Gibbs properties, pressure, Gibbs measures for potentials, and equilibrium states, the talk states that B-free measures of maximal entropy need not be Gibbs but that one-coordinate potentials still admit an explicit pressure formula and a unique equilibrium measure.

proof_stages.label: Two-coordinate pressure formula
proof_stages.summary: For nondegenerate nearest-neighbor potentials, transfer-matrix intuition from the full shift is replaced by periodic B-approximants, whose zero-separated block decomposition expresses pressure via density and Mirsky frequencies of blocks of zeros followed by runs of ones.

proof_stages.label: High-density asymptotics for the remaining pressure term
proof_stages.summary: Within the family of Erdős sets, bounds on Mirsky block frequencies and reciprocal sums are used to split the relevant series and prove that the third pressure term is O((1-d)^epsilon) as the density d tends to one, with the general case left open.

examples.name: Square-free integers, B={p^2}
examples.note: The square-free case is presented as the original model: it is the Möbius-square factor that led to the general B-free program and to general questions about proximality, entropy, and failure of the Gibbs property.
examples.engagement_reason: Square-free integers are used as a central cited example with stated properties such as density, entropy, genericity, and proximality, but no concrete calculation is carried out on that specific case in the transcript.

passes.reason: A standard Erdős B-free example, later cited through the square-free MME's failure of the Gibbs property.

examples.name: Prime and semiprime B-free systems
examples.note: Taking B to be primes or semiprimes illustrates how Behrend-type B-free systems quickly lead to hard questions such as the cardinality of the prime subshift.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker concretely identifies the B-free numbers for B equal to primes as just ±1 and for semiprimes as essentially the primes, using the definition on these examples.

passes.reason: For primes or semiprimes the multiples have density one and the B-free set is finite or essentially prime-sparse.
For B equal to primes or semiprimes the B-free set collapses to units or essentially primes, a zero-density Behrend endpoint used to warn against easy general theory.
prime and semiprime choices give zero-density B-free sets, with the prime case nearly trivial, and are used as Behrend enemy cases for general theory
For B equal to primes or semiprimes the B-free set is finite or essentially the primes, a zero-density Behrend boundary case with collapsed measure-theoretic behavior.
